name: AutoShape 26
text: 1 / 10 - 5-Minute Thesis Report
textPreview: 1 / 10 - 5-Minute Thesis Report

text: Hello everyone. Today, I'll present our project on "Style-Aware Adaptation for Language-Conditioned Humanoid Motion Generation." Our goal was to enhance a state-of-the-art model, MoConVQ, to understand and generate specific motion styles from text prompts. We faced a key challenge: the lack of direct supervision data. Our solution was to use a technique called LoRA distillation, which allowed us to adapt the model with minimal trainable parameters—just 1.97 million, or about 1% of the total. Despite this efficiency, we achieved significant success, with 18 out of 22 proxy metrics showing the desired style change.

name: AutoShape 31
text: 2 / 8 - Motivation
textPreview: 2 / 8 - Motivation

text: Let's start with the motivation. Using natural language to control robots is intuitive, and MoConVQ is a leading model for this. However, it's not good at understanding style—like making a walk "slow and deliberate" versus "energetic." Our goal was to fix this, but with a catch: we couldn't retrain the entire massive model. So, we focused on parameter-efficient adaptation. Our key contributions were reproducing the baseline, innovating a new LoRA distillation method, and thoroughly evaluating our results using multiple metrics.

textPreview: Source: HumanML3D Dataset Analysis; Knowledge Distillation Methodology

name: AutoShape 33
text: 3 / 8 - Data Challenge & Solution
textPreview: 3 / 8 - Data Challenge & Solution

text: A major hurdle was the data. We used the HumanML3D dataset, but there was a critical mismatch: MoConVQ expects discrete "tokens" as training targets, but the dataset only provides continuous motion data. Converting between them is difficult and error-prone. Our elegant solution was knowledge distillation. We used the pre-trained MoConVQ model as a teacher to generate the necessary pseudo-tokens for our style prompts. Then, our LoRA-adapted student model learned to copy the teacher. This bypassed the data conversion problem entirely.

name: AutoShape 28

textPreview: Source: LoRA Adaptation Pipeline Methodology & Evaluation Framework

name: AutoShape 29
text: 4 / 8 - Final Method
textPreview: 4 / 8 - Final Method

text: Here's a visual overview of our entire method. The process starts with style captions from the HumanML3D dataset. These are fed into the frozen MoConVQ teacher model, which generates the pseudo-tokens used as training targets. The student model, which is MoConVQ with LoRA adapters, is trained to predict these tokens. Crucially, only the LoRA parameters are updated. After training, the adapted student generates tokens that are then passed through the frozen decoder and controller to produce the final motion files (BVH). We evaluate the results by checking if the student learned the tokens, and by measuring changes in the generated motion itself.

name: AutoShape 31
text: 5 / 8 - Experimental Setup
textPreview: 5 / 8 - Experimental Setup

text: Now, let's look at our experimental setup. We used the official MoConVQ model and trained on 200 style captions. Our LoRA setup was very efficient, training just 1.97 million parameters, which is about 1% of the generator. The training involved an initial phase followed by a refinement phase with a lower learning rate. For evaluation, we tracked the training loss, used a set of custom "proxy metrics" derived from the motion files to measure style, and also did visual checks by rendering animations.

name: AutoShape 27
text: 6 / 8 - Training Evidence
textPreview: 6 / 8 - Training Evidence

text: Let's look at the results. First, the training loss. As you can see from the graph, the loss consistently decreased, which is a strong indicator that our LoRA adapter was successfully learning to mimic the teacher model. Beyond the numbers, we also saw clear visual differences. For example, when given a "slow deliberate walk" prompt, our model produced a noticeably different, more crouched gait compared to the baseline. These qualitative results give us confidence that the model is indeed learning the intended styles.

name: AutoShape 20
text: 7 / 8 - Proxy Results
textPreview: 7 / 8 - Proxy Results

text: For a more quantitative measure, we used our proxy metrics. The results were very positive. Out of 22 predefined checks across 5 different styles, our model moved in the desired direction 18 times, a win rate of over 80%. As the chart shows, it performed perfectly on styles like "slow deliberate" and "heavy tired," which have clear kinematic signatures. The most challenging style was "Tai Chi," which highlights a limitation: styles requiring more complex, coordinated movements are harder to capture with simple proxies.

name: AutoShape 30
text: 8 / 8 - Interpretation & Next Steps
textPreview: 8 / 8 - Interpretation & Next Steps

text: So, what do these results mean? Our approach is solid for controlling styles that have clear physical correlates, like speed or smoothness. The distillation method was a technically sound solution to our data problem. However, limitations exist. We still lack direct token labels for true supervised training, and we couldn't use standard evaluation metrics. Also, our model can't create new motions the original MoConVQ doesn't know. The best next step is to build a better bridge between the model and standard benchmarks, moving beyond proxies.
